title: House Robber II
description:
## House Robber II

You are planning to rob houses along a circular street. Each house has a certain amount of money, given by the array `nums`. Adjacent houses have a connected alarm — robbing two directly neighboring houses will trigger it.

Because the street is circular, the first and last houses are also neighbors. Return the maximum amount of money you can rob without triggering any alarms.

**Constraints:**
- `1 <= nums.length <= 100`
- `0 <= nums[i] <= 1000`

examples.explanation: Cannot rob house 0 and house 2 (neighbors). Best is house 1 alone: 3.
Rob house 1 (2) alone, or house 2 (3) alone. Best is 3.

hiddenCases.explanation: Both adjacent in circle; rob either
Rob alternating houses skipping circular constraint
Houses 0 and 4 are adjacent in circle. Best: rob house 0 and house 3 = 1001, or house 1 and house 4 = 1001.

solutions.label: Two Linear Passes — O(n)
solutions.explanation: Because houses are arranged in a circle, the first and last house are adjacent and cannot both be robbed. This reduces the problem to two independent linear House Robber subproblems: one excluding the first house (`nums[1:]`), and one excluding the last house (`nums[:-1]`). The answer is the maximum of the two. The linear rob function uses two variables to track the best solution up to the previous two houses.

solutions.label: DP with Arrays — O(n)
solutions.explanation: Same two-pass logic as the optimal, but uses an explicit dp array instead of two rolling variables. `dp[i]` represents the maximum money that can be robbed from the first `i+1` houses. The transition is `dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + houses[i])`. Uses O(n) space instead of O(1).

solutions.label: Two Linear Passes — O(n)
solutions.explanation: Since the houses are circular, either the first or last house is excluded. Run the linear rob twice on nums[0..n-2] and nums[1..n-1], return the max.

hints: The circular constraint means you can't rob both the first and last house. Split into two cases: one where you exclude the first house, one where you exclude the last.
Each case is just the original linear House Robber problem — solve it with DP in O(n).
